era_context: The Iron Age II in the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The Neo-Assyrian Empire under Shalmaneser III is in its expansionist phase, conducting annual military campaigns. In Greece, the Archaic period is underway; city-states are consolidating power and beginning colonization. In China, the Western Zhou dynasty is in decline, approaching the Spring and Autumn period. Egypt is fragmented under the late 21st Dynasty.

events.title: Shalmaneser III's military campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Shalmaneser III of Assyria conducted one of his annual campaigns into the Levant, consolidating Assyrian dominance over the region. These campaigns, documented in the Monolith Inscription and other royal annals, were part of a systematic expansion that would define his 35-year reign. The campaigns targeted both tribute collection and military subjugation of local kingdoms, establishing Assyrian hegemony that would persist for a century.

sources.name: Monolith Inscription of Shalmaneser III (Kurkh Stele)

events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser III's reign is well-documented through contemporary royal inscriptions. Annual campaigns are attested; specific year assignment follows Assyrian chronology, which has ±2 year uncertainty in this period.

events.title: Phoenician maritime expansion and trade networks
events.description: Phoenician city-states, particularly Tyre and Sidon, were at the height of their maritime commercial power during this period. Archaeological evidence and later Greek sources attest to extensive trade networks connecting the Levantine coast with Egypt, Cyprus, Greece, and beyond. This era saw the consolidation of Phoenician commercial dominance that would define the Iron Age Mediterranean economy.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Phoenician port sites (Tyre, Sidon)

events.certainty_note: Phoenician maritime dominance in this period is well-established archaeologically. Specific events for 819 BCE are not individually documented; this represents the broader context of Phoenician activity in this decade.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of Shalmaneser III's individual campaigns relies on Assyrian king list chronology, which has been revised multiple times. Some scholars place certain campaigns 1–2 years earlier or later than traditional dating. The significance of Phoenician trade in this specific year cannot be isolated from the broader Iron Age II pattern; no single event of 819 BCE stands out as uniquely transformative.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 819 BCE is heavily weighted toward Mesopotamia and the Levant, where written records and inscriptions survive. Greek sources are sparse and retrospective. African, Central Asian, and European history for this year is essentially unattested in surviving sources. Assyrian royal annals are propagandistic and emphasize military success; they may underrepresent diplomatic or economic activity. The Phoenician record is primarily archaeological and indirect.

graph_edges.note: Shalmaneser III's campaigns continued annually throughout his reign; this is one instance in a sustained pattern.
